literal: from √bṛh, 'to grow, to swell, to expand'; the swelling-without-bound, the encompassing reality
invariant_definition: The ultimate reality of which the Upaniṣads speak, the *tātparya* (settled purport) of all Vedānta inquiry, the cause (in some sense — efficient, material, or both) of the world, and that whose knowledge is liberation. Every Vedāntic school agrees that *brahman* is the unique reality referred to by *Brahma-Sūtra* 1.1.2 (*janmādy asya yataḥ*), that it is *sat-cit-ānanda* (existence-consciousness-bliss), and that it is the unique object whose realization frees from *saṃsāra*. Schools diverge sharply on whether *brahman* is *nirviśeṣa* (without internal differentiation) or *saviśeṣa* (qualified by real internal differentiation), on whether *brahman* is the only reality or one reality among others ontologically dependent on it, and on whether *brahman* is best characterized impersonally (as *nirguṇa*) or personally (as the Lord, *Bhagavān*, identified with Viṣṇu, Śiva, or Kṛṣṇa).

per_school.definition: *Brahman* is the sole reality (*ekam evādvitīyam*, Chāndogya 6.2.1). Properly described, *brahman* is *nirguṇa* (without determinate qualities) and *nirviśeṣa* (without internal differentiation); the *saguṇa brahman* of devotional practice is a lower (*apara*) standpoint, valid for the seeker but sublatable in *brahma-jñāna*. *Brahman* is identical with *ātman* (mahāvākya *tat tvam asi*, Chāndogya 6.8.7). The world's apparent emergence from *brahman* is *vivarta* (apparent transformation), not *pariṇāma* (real transformation): *brahman* is not modified.

*Brahman* is *saviśeṣa* — eternally qualified by the totality of conscious selves (*cit*) and inert matter (*acit*) as His body (*śarīra*). *Nirguṇa* texts are read as denying *heya-guṇas* (defects), not all attributes. *Brahman* is identified with Viṣṇu/Nārāyaṇa, the personal Lord with infinite auspicious qualities (*ananta-kalyāṇa-guṇa*). The relation of *brahman* to *cit* and *acit* is *aprthak-siddhi* (inseparable establishment) — neither identity nor mere conjunction.

*Brahman* is the unique *svatantra* (independent) reality, identified strictly with Viṣṇu. All else — *jīvas* and inert matter — is *paratantra* (dependent). The five real distinctions (*pañca-bheda*) — between *brahman* and *jīva*, *brahman* and matter, *jīva* and *jīva*, *jīva* and matter, matter and matter — are eternal and ontologically primitive. *Brahman* possesses infinite real qualities; the *nirguṇa* texts deny only *prākṛta-guṇas* (the qualities of inert matter).

*Brahman* is simultaneously identical with and different from the world; the difference is not illusory and the identity is not nominal. Bhāskara holds that *brahman* really transforms (*pariṇāma*) into the world without losing its essence — *vivarta-vāda* is rejected as a Buddhist smuggling. The *upādhi* (limiting condition) of embodiment is real, not illusory.

*Brahman* is simultaneously and inconceivably (*acintya*) one with and different from its *śaktis* (powers, including jīvas and matter). The relation is *acintya* because logic cannot resolve it — it is a real metaphysical fact disclosed by *śabda*, not a contradiction. *Brahman* is identified with Kṛṣṇa (the *svayaṃ-bhagavān*), of whom impersonal *brahman* is the bodily effulgence (*brahmajyoti*) and *paramātman* the indwelling aspect.

*Brahman* is identified with Kṛṣṇa, who is *sat-cit-ānanda* in His full nature. The world is a real *avikṛta-pariṇāma* (transformation without modification) of *brahman* — a transformation that produces real effects without diminishing the source. There is no *māyā* in the Advaita sense; the world's reality is undiminished.

*Brahman* is naturally both one with and different from the *jīva* and the world; the difference and the identity are equally real and equally primary, not derived from each other. *Brahman* is identified with Kṛṣṇa accompanied by Rādhā.

translator_note: *Brahman* is conventionally translated 'the Absolute' or 'God', both misleading. 'The Absolute' carries Hegelian and German-idealist freight that does not belong to the Sanskrit; 'God' implies a personal creator-deity, which is true only on some Vedāntic readings (Viśiṣṭādvaita, Dvaita, the Vaiṣṇava bhedābheda schools) and explicitly denied as the highest description on others (Advaita's *nirguṇa brahman*). The root sense — 'that which expands without bound' — is closer to the Sanskrit. Note also: *brahman* (neuter) ≠ *brahmā* (masculine, the four-faced creator-deity of the Trimūrti), ≠ *brāhmaṇa* (member of the priestly varṇa). English script collapses all three; Sanskrit does not.
